speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two, with Irving Eisner. Mr. Eisner, you mentioned that, in the early 1940s, you sensed a change in the attitude of the Hungarian non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will even tell you some cases, which is very, very clear what happened. First of all, one of my uncles-- my father's sister married to a Polish man, a Jewish beautiful man, Moshe Avraham. He was from Galicia. We lived 20 kilometers from the border. In fact, in time of Czechoslovakia, we were so friendly with the Czechs that my father and mother used to go for a weekend to Lvov, to Lemberk, with the Czech soldiers and officers, just to have a weekend out. We were very, very friendly. And yet, in 1940 or maybe '41, he was taken away already by the Hungarian Gendarmerie, with the feathers in their hats, because they found out he was from Poland. They took him back to Lemberk, in that vicinity, in 1940. And we never heard about it. Also, another case, how they started, the Hungarians already. For [INAUDIBLE] for Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, we had a [NON-ENGLISH]. He came to daven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at the time that you [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: In [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to daven for us. He was a Melamed, a teacher in our Heder. But to make a few dollars, whatever the money was. And actually, he stayed in our place, because my father paid him the money. There was no people to-- to pay for him. I remember he lived about 15 kilometers over a hill, over a mountain, in a village even smaller than ours. OK, you imagine how big it was. And he came to [? Olfasten ?] to break his fast to our place, and we ate a good supper. We had cabbage sauce, I remember. My mother made a good meal. And my father gave him some money and said to him, Chaim. His name was Chaim [INAUDIBLE]. He says, stay. Tomorrow morning, you will go home. And he said, no, I didn't see my wife and my children. I'm going tonight home. And he left our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a day later, the wife came back asking, what happened to my husband? Is he still here? Said, what [INAUDIBLE] here? He left yesterday night. He never arrived. So the Hungarians, while he was walking in the street, they took him into their wagons. They took him across the river, and they shot him there. And we found a few days later his body. So Hungarians began already to poison-- to poison the villagers, to show them that we are different. We are people who look down on us. And they start to give them the permission to do things which were never done before.

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, when I was riding my bicycle to the second village, to a bigger-- to a higher school, they began to throw stones already on me [INAUDIBLE] lately. And slowly, slowly, I was afraid to ride the bicycle five kilometers, because they might hit me. And there was no one to stop them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the time regularly became worse, and worse, and worse, until the villagers themselves-- they still didn't have the chutzpahs, the guts, openly to begin to attack us. But the Hungarians began to build in the hate against the Jewish people. And as year passed by, '42 became worse. '43, even worse, until it came to-- to liquidation of the whole village. The first liquidation was in 1941, as I recall. People didn't have Hungarian citizenship. Why should we have Hungarian citizenship? We were grown up by the Czechs. That was an excuse.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, with lots of money, he went down to Budapest. And for money, he bought himself papers. So he was rescued. And men were-- women and children were rescued if their husbands were already in the military. All the rest were taken away already to Galicia, to-- I know the names all by heart because we received some use from them-- is for three places-- Horodenka, Kolomyia-- Horodenka, Kolomyia, and one more place, where they were thrown right into the Dnieper, into the Dniester. It was a river. Right from the trucks, they were thrown into the rivers. Our people, our villagers. We were left only-- out of 60 families, about five were left in the village, til 1944. 1944, they took everybody, including us. So that was the end of the village. So the antisemitism increased from day after day, day after day, because the Hungarians, in many, many cases, they were even worse than the Germans. Because they were peasants. They were nothing since the beginning. All of a sudden, they became rulers. And you know, when a person like this becomes-- becomes a ruler, [NON-ENGLISH]. Because I've said it many times-- a human being, if he turns into an animal, he's worse than an animal. Because an animal-- you take a lion or a tiger, give him a piece of bread, he will sit down beside you. But if a human being becomes an animal, he has a method how to kill, how to torture. You can't stop him. It's even worse. In my words I once said it, last time, David-- David [? HaMelech, ?] King David, when he was in trouble, and he said to God, please, dear God, don't let me fall into the hands of a human being if I have to be punished. If I have to be punished, you take care of me. You, God, because you have more passion-- patience. You have more [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I wrote it down. [NON-ENGLISH] Don't let me fall into the hands of a man. And I witnessed it. And I saw it. That's why, my [NON-ENGLISH], I always said to God, if I have to be punished-- you are punishing me. Keep on doing it. Keep on doing it. But you do it. Don't let me be gassed or killed by the-- by-- by the [NON-ENGLISH]. If I have to be punished, fine. You do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what happened. It became worse and worse, and we began to become dehumanized. We began-- we started to be not human at all, slowly, slowly, until the same villagers-- the same who were really my best boyfriends from school. They began, now, to take me to the train, to go to-- to Birkenau. How can you translate this? Were any anti-Jewish measures, like, for instance, the wearing of the star? Did that come into effect in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came up, yes. It came in 1944. When we went to the ghetto in April, we had to put on-- in the ghetto, we had to put on our stars-- yellow stars. Yes. And we went with those stars til Birkenau. We had those stars. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll deal with that also, a little bit later. During these years before the invasion of the Germans, did you hear about what was happening to the Polish Jews? The German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- because again, maybe my father did, maybe my mother did, but we didn't talk about it. We had a radio, I remember, with a secret channel. Underground, it was called. He could hear news from London, England. And we knew about the war. We didn't know so much. We knew, in Poland, the Jews are suffering, the Jews are being killed. But what method of killing, we didn't know. In fact, our family-- I myself, didn't know till literally seeing the crematoria and the gas chambers. Till that moment, I didn't know what's waiting for us. We didn't know. Maybe the adults did. I-- we kids, I mean, we were young, we didn't know about it. What is waiting, we didn't know. We knew is coming hard times, because Hungarians are antisemites, and the Germans are for sure. But we did not-- we were not involved in any politics. Why, and how, and when, and what's going on. We didn't know-- very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what you've told us, your town was very small. Was there any Zionist activity or Zionist groups at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- again, what I remember as a child-- childhood, I remember there was a lit-- a girl, about 22, 23. Her name was Pirha. Pirha-- flower. Pirha. And I remember, like now, seeing her assembling, gathering the young teenagers, and singing with them some Hebrew songs. And-- and I was told later on, she was from the [INAUDIBLE] party, from, like, [NON-ENGLISH]. They used to sing-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH]. I didn't belong to any of those, but I remember them singing. And I sang with them. Even though my father wouldn't let me to belong, because the rabbi of the villages, of our villages, would never agree. Said, my father's son or daughter should belong to a Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from the distance, I heard that she used to go from village to village. Once a month, she arrived to our village. I remember like today the Shabbos. She assembled the kids, and she was singing with them Hebrew songs. What it meant, I don't know. [LAUGHS] The rabbi who was in charge of your region, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was [INAUDIBLE] Aharon-- Aharon Teitelbaum. They are from-- they were, as today, as known the [? Sackmeuller ?] dynasty. He was a great man, but they were anti-Zionists. Why? I was too young to understand. They were anti-Zionists. They-- they wouldn't-- they wouldn't even hardly talk to a person who went to one of those Zionist groups. Or like myself, I had difficulties to go to Hebrew school. The only way-- I mean, my father went to the rabbi to ask, can he send me and my sisters, the biggest-- I mean, two years younger sister-- to go to a Zionist school. The answer was, no. No way. In fact, they threatened my father, put him into a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means isolation, out of the camp, the Jewish camp. But finally, my uncle-- my-- my mother's brother-- who was in the [YIDDISH], he was going into these Zionistic groups, he kind of backed my father to let me go to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the only condition was that I can go, but I must come every four weeks to my rabbi, to his office, and he is going to test me. I must learn the same Talmud, the same everything what they learn it in the yeshiva. And I must have my payos. So I put my payos under my ears, because it was a Zionist school. And I had to come home and go to the rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH], and he was testing me exactly what they learned in the yeshiva in Munkacs. But I could not go into the yeshiva, into the courtyard. They wouldn't let me. And they would stone me, probably. I couldn't go in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had to learn the same. In fact, my father, as I mentioned, he has the money. He hired for me a tutor. It was a right hand of the rabbi of Munkacs. And he taught me the same-- the same material as they learned in yeshiva. That was the only way I could to go to school. But it didn't last long. I was there only-- not even two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your mother's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- had brought you into the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because he was more Zionistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avraham. Avraham Katz. My mother's name was Katz. That was his real--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her real brother. Yeah. Avraham Katz And he very ear-- very long [INAUDIBLE] earlier. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: But he survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived. He was like a soldier and-- with the yellow band, you know, in Hungarian army. Yeah, he survived. He survived. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish soldiers were allowed to serve in the Hungarian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not-- not trusted with a rifle or with ammunition. They had their-- they were only like workers to help out digging ditches, and supplying food, and so on. They had to wear a yellow band with a special cap. I wanted to go myself, but I was too young. They wouldn't accept me. They had to-- you had to be 19 or over, or so. And secondly, I hesitated even because my father wasn't with us when we were taken to the-- to Birkenau. And I-- and I said to myself, I am the oldest in the family. Maybe I should go with my mother. Maybe, you know, from time to time she needs me to help her. But I wanted-- one time, my mother told me to go into this-- it was called Hungarian munkaszolgálat, working battalion. But I was too young. They didn't accept me. And he was there, my uncle. And he survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] See, next-- next to the torturing of the death camps, like Auschwitz, Birkenau, Majdanck, and so on, so on, the next step of torturing, and also leading to death, were those Hungarian munkaszolgálat, battalions of working. Very few survived from there either. They-- they-- they died from hunger, or from dirt, or from freezing, who knows what. But they lived longer-- more had a chance to survive than in the camps. But there was al-- that was also one method how to get rid of the Jews. Hard labor, dirty labor, pulling wagons in the middle of the winter without shoes in the snow. Because I used to watch them through the window, how they pulled wagons with rocks, without shoes, in the winter, in the snow and the mud. It was torturing. And that was also a method how to-- to get rid of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came back home in 1944?

speaker: interviewer
text: With the invasion of the Germans in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: The invasion of the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. When the Germans invaded Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. No, no, no, no. What happened was, in 1939, September, the Germans invaded Czechoslovakia. And the Czechs had-- they went to fight. They-- my father was called into the army. I remember it like today. It was before Rosh Hashanah, [INAUDIBLE] Rosh Hashanah Yom Kippur. And didn't last long. It lasted three weeks or so. They surrendered. And that's when Czechoslovakia was split into two parts, one part Hungarian-- they gave it to Hungary, because they were together-- and one part to Germany. My part was gone, was given to the Hungarians. That's when the Hungarians marched in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was end of Czechoslovakia. Hungarians marched in there. And then, we were with Hungarians till April, 194-- March, end of March, 1944, when the Germans came in finally to this part of the country too. That's when they liquidated every Jew that was still left, for some reasons. Everybody had to go. Describe to us the events as they happened in April, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: In April, 1944, my father was taken away before us, because he was a leader of the village and he was rich. Until the Hungarians were ruling, he paid them off. He bribed them. And because of money, he saved lots of Jews. The Hungarians looked away. Like I said, in the beginning, we delivered them to Budapest and so on. We saved as much as we can, as many as we can. But in 19-- in March, end of March, April beginning-- the Germans came in, and my father couldn't do a thing. And some of those Hungarians went and told about him, what he did all the time. And they came and they took him away. The Germans took him away. The Hungarians took away to the Germans, to Budapest. And he was the first transport with Hungarian Jews to go to the camps, not knowing where they're going. But we-- he had some knowledge because in 1941, my [NON-ENGLISH], Nathan was taken away with his family. And in the ghetto, where I was with my mother, the owner of that property, he was a [NON-ENGLISH] The committee of the Jews was outside the city. And somehow, a little card arrived to their office from my father, from somewhere from Poland. And I remember, he assembled us as a ghetto, and he read the little card. And my father wrote the following, [NON-ENGLISH] which means, I was-- I went to see my father-in-law. I couldn't find him, but it's very hot over there. It's like fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the word fire was mentioned. It's hot over there. And we started to speculate, what does he means? He went to see my [NON-ENGLISH], his father-in-law. He didn't find him, and it's boiling over there. That was the card. Still, no one believed what that hot means, what the fire means. We all knew we're going to be resettled into Hungary to go to work. That's when we started to realize what's happened. So what happened? My father was taken. As a first transport from Budapest, he was taken to Poland. And-- What was that parting between your father and your mother-- was-- what was that like? When he was taken away, how did your mother react?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still in school. I wasn't home. I was still in Munkacs. And when I came home, that was the first time in my life I traveled Friday night on a Sabbath. Because we were five students, three girls and two boys, from [INAUDIBLE] going in Munkacs to school. And we came to go home. Because one morning, you went to school in Munkacs, and our principal, Dr. Marvoy-- he didn't survive-- what I heard, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was staying outside and telling us, boys and girls, go home. There's no more school. And we saw the windows were broken. Jews cannot go to school anymore. Go home. I will send your report cards home. That was the last time I saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So five of us-- we were from better-- richer-- richer families, but who could afford to go board and room? Only people that has the money. So we went to-- to the train to go home. Friday, you have a choice. We came to the train. They told us, Jews cannot go by train no more. But we want to go home to our parents. No way.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- sorry, that was Thursday. So Friday morning, one of our teachers endangered his life. He said he is going to the office of the Hungarians to ask a permit to let us go home. He's going to-- to tell that we are from Chust and want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, they gave us a permit to go home. And we traveled by train. We were looking one of another. Nobody should see us, that we are Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to Chust. They went to their homes. I went to my house. I came to-- to my house. It's Friday night. It's quiet. It's late at night. I knocked at the door. My mother was up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Naturally, the first thing I say, [NON-ENGLISH], where is my father? She says, [YIDDISH]. He's sleeping. Don't wake him up. It's not nice. I said, no, I want to give him a kiss. [INAUDIBLE] I didn't see him a long time. No, don't bother. Tomorrow morning, you will go to shul. He will wake you up. Don't worry. But I said, no, no. I want to give a kiss. I went to kiss my brothers and sisters, you know? And then she broke out with tears. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Your father is not here. I said, what do you mean? She says, he's arrested in the village. In Sojmy he went for business. Sunday morning, they will take him to Budapest. So the bus will arrive 9:00 to Chust. And from Chust, 5:00 the afternoon, he will have a train to go to Budapest, a whole night train to go to the most secure prison in Hungary, in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was called [INAUDIBLE]. Those who are from Hungary will know what I mean. My wife, Hungarian, she knows what it is. Anyway, 9:00, we waited for my father. I went. The little kids didn't go. My mother came with me to the train. My father came, tied down to a tall, tall Hungarian officer-- police. And the police told my father, Mr. Eisner, I can let you go. But if you listen to me, you'd better go to the jail. It's much more secure. Because in the streets, they will kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while we're waiting for the train-- it's a funny story. It was a long story. I used to be in the Hungarian youth group. Had to be, not because I wanted. We used to have a military cap and a yellow band. And we used to go to work-- 16, 17, 18 years old boys-- to work for the Hungarians, to chop wood for the-- for heating, to clean the snow. Who knows what? And while I was with my father, he says to me, go home. Bring me your band and bring me your military cap. I said, [? Tata, ?] what do you need it for? [YIDDISH], he said. Don't ask questions. Go and bring it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran home. It was pretty far, the house to the train station. My mother is with him. And I bring-- I brought it to him. And then, they try, you know, to take him out. He paid those Hungarians, but it didn't work. Before the train came, he told me. He says, the reason I'm taking it with me, I am planning to escape. And if I-- if they catch me, I will tell them I belong to a regiment, but we were shot on and we were spreading. And I am here-- I have the cap. I have the band. So maybe this will help him. And I remember-- it's very sad-- the train begin to arrive, and he said the following. I've never forgot this. He says, [YIDDISH], he said, Ich will bleiben [? leben. ?] I will stay alive. [YIDDISH]. I am afraid what will happen to you and to your children. That's what he said. And we left. And the next station-- we left home. He was taken to-- the train station-- [INAUDIBLE] in the book. I'm writing about it. He says, they were asking him to go to bring in two pails of water to the-- to the wagon, to have water. And he went with police, because the police just went with him to the house. He went to the house. And he had some money over there. He paid for a Hungarian guy, and he gave him a pliers to cut wires, and a screwdriver. And he smuggled it in. Into the water of the pails, he put it. He brought it in, because his idea was to escape. And the night came, and he told some people, listen. I know that we are going to be taken to death-- because he has a mail, he wrote us. He didn't know what there, but he knew we are going to death. Let's break the door. I can open the door, he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the Hungarians and the Germans were in the last wagon, sitting, drinking beer, having fun, you know? That what he told me, that he heard them singing when he jumped from the train. And the Hungarians, no way. Said we are Hungarians, and then we are Jewish. For our Fatherland, for our country, we cannot be traitors.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't let him. He says, you are from the Carpathians. You are from Czechoslovakia. You don't belong to us. You don't dare. We're going tell about you. Nightfall, and they-- my father doesn't know what to do. Finally, a young couple came to him, just married. And they told him, Mr. Eisner, quietly, we are willing to do it. And the woman had a beautiful ring, diamond. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they split up the three diamonds among themselves. And my father-- came to jumping, they didn't want-- as my father jumped out from the train the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you back in the village? You were in Chust at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Chust. But a few weeks later, about three weeks later, we went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how was this accomplished?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Hungarians? Were the Germans in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The Hungarians were in charge. So they came and told us-- there were three ghettos in Chust, and they divided the city. Our part belonged to ghetto two. And they told us when to be ready. We have to move ourself there. In other words, we hired a little-- a Christian with a wagon, with a horse, to take our little belongings. And we went to a ghetto where my father used to make business with that man. He was on the committee-- Jewish committee. And we went to his ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to the ghetto right after Pesach, 1944. My father was not anymore with us for Pesach. I made the Seder. It was a Seder, more crying than the Seder. And right after Pesach, we went to the ghetto. And the end of May, we arrived to Birkenau. My father jumped the train the first time. Tell me. The ghetto, was it a fenced ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hardly recall. It was not particularly made with a fence. There were some fences from the people living around, but the Hungarians did not put up a fence. It was not a built fence. No. But there were guards by the entrance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a curfew? You were not allowed access in and out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was given permission with a special band to go out twice a week for shopping with the Hungarian police. But you had-- you had a-- you needed a special permission to go out. And we had to return-- I don't remember the time exactly, but it was a-- it was a curfew. It was a curfew. What bothered me so terribly-- when I went out in the city in Chust to buy products, I saw trucks loaded with Hungarian and German soldiers escaping from the battle field, which was 80 kilometers in a place called, [? Yassina. ?] It was a border between Galicia and Czechoslovakia. If the Russians would have moved five, six hours, they would have captured us and we would have stayed home. And for some political reasons, they stopped at the border. They didn't move. And during those few weeks, till they started to move on, Eichmann succeeded to take away 250,000 Jews from Hungary. If they would have moved-- I saw them only wounded, running away from the battlefield. They stopped at the border, the Russians. And we were just 80 kilometers away. And nothing-- they didn't move for some reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of the ghetto in Chust.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was supervised by [INAUDIBLE] Gendarmerie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they supervised it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were conditions like in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conditions were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very, very poor. They put us, I remember-- I speak about my own case, my family-- they put us into one room, maybe 10 feet long and maybe 12 wide. We were four families there on one floor. Everybody had one corner. Everybody had one corner. And that's how we lived there. And we started to sew-- to make our stars, to be ready to go to the train. And we still had food. We had some food brought with us. And we were allowed, like I said, to bring in some-- the Jewish-- the Jewish committee gave the money to buy some food for the people. But even from the ghetto, they were taken-- the young boys and young men-- with trucks to go to work for the Hungarians in the forest, to build bunkers for ammunition and so on, and at night, we were brought back. So it was very poor conditions. We were about five-- five weeks in the ghetto, altogether. But we had a meal every morning. The Chust rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH] he survived. His name was Shia Greenwald. Also from [? Sackmeuller ?] dynasty. I used to make him, every morning, a meal. And 1944, [NON-ENGLISH], which is beginning of June, [NON-ENGLISH] end of May, we were davening. And they took-- they brought through a ghetto from a village. They threw in stones into our windows, and we couldn't finish our prayers. After that, he said to me, [INAUDIBLE], come over. Bring a scissors with you. Please come and help me cut my beard.
